revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|33.806823|135.64888}}|caption=Kii Road (around the Daitoyama Mountains area)|width=256}} '''Kii Road''' ({{ill|Kii Road|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Road|1=Kiiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604965}}, formerly also known as '''Kiro'''〈{{ill|Kiro|ja|紀路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}〉) is one of the pilgrimage routes of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and a branch of the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]].
It begins at the mouth of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodogawa]] at {{ill|Watanabetsu|ja|渡辺津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabetsu|1=Watanabe no Tsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562382}} (in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]), heading straight south, passing through [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], crossing the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and from [[:en:Tanabe, Wakayama|Tanabe City]] (displayed as Kii Tanabe) proceeding east along the peripheral area of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountains|ja|大塔山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountains|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} until reaching [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes lead to both [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
Until the early modern period, the section east of Kii Tanabe was known as the '''Nakahechi''' ({{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}}) (see [[#中辺路|later description]]), but this article deals with the route as a whole.
== Overview == [[File:Map Kumano Kodo (ja).png|thumb|right|300px|Map of the Kumano Kodo. The light blue and yellow lines indicate the Kii Road]] The Kii Road is a pilgrimage route linking the [[:en:Kinai|Kinai]] region with the Kumano Sanzan. Among the Kumano Kodo routes, it is one of the oldest known—together with the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|1=Iseji Route|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379139}}—as celebrated in the [[:en:Ryōjin Hishō|Ryōjin Hisho]]. However, due to the constraints imposed by the {{ill|Izumi Mountains|ja|和泉山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumi Mountains|1=Izumi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417784}} and the {{ill|Kongō Range|ja|葛城山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongō Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Settsu and Izumi, and by the [[:en:Kii Mountains|Kii Mountains]] and its originating rivers in Kii Province, the route was far from easy.
Despite these difficulties, from the 7th century onward the Kii Road was formally recognized as a pilgrimage route to the Kumano Sanzan, witnessing a flourishing of pilgrimages by the retired emperors of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and the aristocracy. Moreover, during the late [[:en:平安|Heian period]] to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|1=Kamakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939227}}—when medieval Kumano pilgrimages were undertaken principally on foot and included visits to the {{ill|Kujūkuni Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuni Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}—ascetic practitioners (【修験者】) led the way by organizing the routes and instructing in pilgrimage rituals, thus endowing the arduous journey with profound religious significance.
Medieval Kumano pilgrimages eventually came to an end when the institutions that had been the main drivers of these journeys—the retired emperors and the powerful Kumano Betto families who had long governed the Kumano Sanzan with imperial backing—declined following the 【Jōkyū War】. Thereafter, as the principal pilgrims shifted to eastern warriors and influential farmers, the character of the Kii Road changed; it gradually lost its status as the main route and was eventually incorporated into the network of the [[:en:Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. Although Kumano pilgrimages did not disappear entirely, the elaborate rituals of the medieval period were lost, and alternative branch routes were established to bypass particularly difficult sections. In the regions of Settsu and Izumi, the Kii Road was also known as the {{ill|Kumano Kaidō|ja|熊野街道|1=Kumano Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568976}}, serving as a main thoroughfare for local residents and playing significant political and economic roles. In modern times, when national road networks were established, the Kii Road’s original route was often followed.
The Kii Road, which set out from Watanabetsu at the mouth of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodogawa]] in Settsu Province, runs nearly parallel to the {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} along a line of spring water emerging from the foothills of the Izumi Hills, before turning over the hills near what is now [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]. It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. Crossing the [[:en:Japan Median Tectonic Line|Central Tectonic Line]] to the south at the Kino River, the terrain derived from the Kii Mountains continues with a series of rivers that originate there—the {{ill|Kino River|ja|紀ノ川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kino River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀の川|1=Kinokawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042624}}, {{ill|Arita River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita River|1=Arida river|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8378912}}, and {{ill|Hidaka River|ja|日高川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka River|1=Hidaka River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11510812}}—until reaching Tanabe. From Tanabe the road heads east; after following the valley of the {{ill|Iwatagawa|ja|岩田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatagawa|1=Iwata River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475480}} and turning over the hills at {{ill|Takijiri|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}}, it continues along the northern edge of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja|大塔山系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to finally arrive at [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes extend for the subsequent pilgrimage to [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
As mentioned above, the Kii Road was not only a path of faith but also a vital route for daily life, and it continued to be used into modern times. However, as easier routes capable of accommodating automobile traffic were developed, many sections that once ran along settlements lost their original form or ceased to exist as proper roads. In 1978 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 53), the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] selected the Kumano Pilgrimage Routes in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], namely the Kii Road, as one of the "100 Selected Historical Routes". This prompted comprehensive investigations into the route and its history. As a result, the portion of the Kii Road east of Tanabe—well preserved in the area of Nakahechi in Tanabe City—has been restored and improved, enabling at least a partial retracing of the original pilgrimage path.
{{translated page|ja|紀伊路|version=104659108|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}
[[ja:紀伊路]]

Moreover, during the late [[:en:平安|Heian period]] to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|1=Kamakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939227}}—when medieval Kumano pilgrimages were undertaken principally on foot and included visits to the {{ill|Kujūkuni Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuni Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}—ascetic practitioners (【修験者】) led the way by organizing the routes and instructing in pilgrimage rituals, thus endowing the arduous journey with profound religious significance.
Medieval Kumano pilgrimages eventually came to an end when the institutions that had been the main drivers of these journeys—the retired emperors and the powerful Kumano Betto families who had long governed the Kumano Sanzan with imperial backing—declined following the 【Jōkyū War】. Thereafter, as the principal pilgrims shifted to eastern warriors and influential farmers, the character of the Kii Road changed; it gradually lost its status as the main route and was eventually incorporated into the network of the [[:en:Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. Although Kumano pilgrimages did not disappear entirely, the elaborate rituals of the medieval period were lost, and alternative branch routes were established to bypass particularly difficult sections. In the regions of Settsu and Izumi, the Kii Road was also known as the {{ill|Kumano Kaidō|ja|熊野街道|1=Kumano Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568976}}, serving as a main thoroughfare for local residents and playing significant political and economic roles. In modern times, when national road networks were established, the Kii Road’s original route was often followed.
The Kii Road, which set out from Watanabetsu at the mouth of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodogawa]] in Settsu Province, runs nearly parallel to the {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} along a line of spring water emerging from the foothills of the Izumi Hills, before turning over the hills near what is now [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]. It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. Crossing the [[:en:Japan Median Tectonic Line|Central Tectonic Line]] to the south at the Kino River, the terrain derived from the Kii Mountains continues with a series of rivers that originate there—the {{ill|Kino River|ja|紀ノ川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kino River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀の川|1=Kinokawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042624}}, {{ill|Arita River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita River|1=Arida river|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8378912}}, and {{ill|Hidaka River|ja|日高川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka River|1=Hidaka River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11510812}}—until reaching Tanabe. From Tanabe the road heads east; after following the valley of the {{ill|Iwatagawa|ja|岩田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatagawa|1=Iwata River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475480}} and turning over the hills at {{ill|Takijiri|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}}, it continues along the northern edge of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja|大塔山系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to finally arrive at [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes extend for the subsequent pilgrimage to [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
As mentioned above, the Kii Road was not only a path of faith but also a vital route for daily life, and it continued to be used into modern times. However, as easier routes capable of accommodating automobile traffic were developed, many sections that once ran along settlements lost their original form or ceased to exist as proper roads. In 1978 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 53), the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] selected the Kumano Pilgrimage Routes in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], namely the Kii Road, as one of the "100 Selected Historical Routes". This prompted comprehensive investigations into the route and its history. As a result, the portion of the Kii Road east of Tanabe—well preserved in the area of Nakahechi in Tanabe City—has been restored and improved, enabling at least a partial retracing of the original pilgrimage path.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|33.806823|135.64888}}|caption=Kii Road (around the Daitoyama Mountains area)|width=256}} '''Kii Road''' ({{ill|Kii Road|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Road|1=Kiiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604965}}, formerly also known as '''Kiro'''〈{{ill|Kiro|ja|紀路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}〉) is one of the pilgrimage routes of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and a branch of the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]].
It begins at the mouth of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodogawa]] at {{ill|Watanabetsu|ja|渡辺津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabetsu|1=Watanabe no Tsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562382}} (in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]), heading straight south, passing through [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], crossing the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and from [[:en:Tanabe, Wakayama|Tanabe City]] (displayed as Kii Tanabe) proceeding east along the peripheral area of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountains|ja|大塔山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountains|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} until reaching [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes lead to both [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
Until the early modern period, the section east of Kii Tanabe was known as the '''Nakahechi''' ({{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}}) (see [[#中辺路|later description]]), but this article deals with the route as a whole.
== Overview == [[File:Map Kumano Kodo (ja).png|thumb|right|300px|Map of the Kumano Kodo. The light blue and yellow lines indicate the Kii Road]] The Kii Road is a pilgrimage route linking the [[:en:Kinai|Kinai]] region with the Kumano Sanzan. Among the Kumano Kodo routes, it is one of the oldest known—together with the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|1=Iseji Route|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379139}}—as celebrated in the [[:en:Ryōjin Hishō|Ryōjin Hisho]]. However, due to the constraints imposed by the {{ill|Izumi Mountains|ja|和泉山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumi Mountains|1=Izumi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417784}} and the {{ill|Kongō Range|ja|葛城山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongō Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Settsu and Izumi, and by the [[:en:Kii Mountains|Kii Mountains]] and its originating rivers in Kii Province, the route was far from easy.
Despite these difficulties, from the 7th century onward the Kii Road was formally recognized as a pilgrimage route to the Kumano Sanzan, witnessing a flourishing of pilgrimages by the retired emperors of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and the aristocracy. Moreover, during the late [[:en:平安|Heian period]] to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|1=Kamakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939227}}—when medieval Kumano pilgrimages were undertaken principally on foot and included visits to the {{ill|Kujūkuni Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuni Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}—ascetic practitioners (【修験者】) led the way by organizing the routes and instructing in pilgrimage rituals, thus endowing the arduous journey with profound religious significance.

Thereafter, as the principal pilgrims shifted to eastern warriors and influential farmers, the character of the Kii Road changed; it gradually lost its status as the main route and was eventually incorporated into the network of the [[:en:Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. Although Kumano pilgrimages did not disappear entirely, the elaborate rituals of the medieval period were lost, and alternative branch routes were established to bypass particularly difficult sections. In the regions of Settsu and Izumi, the Kii Road was also known as the {{ill|Kumano Kaidō|ja|熊野街道|1=Kumano Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568976}}, serving as a main thoroughfare for local residents and playing significant political and economic roles. In modern times, when national road networks were established, the Kii Road’s original route was often followed.
The Kii Road, which set out from Watanabetsu at the mouth of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodogawa]] in Settsu Province, runs nearly parallel to the {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} along a line of spring water emerging from the foothills of the Izumi Hills, before turning over the hills near what is now [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]. It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. Crossing the [[:en:Japan Median Tectonic Line|Central Tectonic Line]] to the south at the Kino River, the terrain derived from the Kii Mountains continues with a series of rivers that originate there—the {{ill|Kino River|ja|紀ノ川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kino River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀の川|1=Kinokawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042624}}, {{ill|Arita River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita River|1=Arida river|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8378912}}, and {{ill|Hidaka River|ja|日高川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka River|1=Hidaka River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11510812}}—until reaching Tanabe. From Tanabe the road heads east; after following the valley of the {{ill|Iwatagawa|ja|岩田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatagawa|1=Iwata River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475480}} and turning over the hills at {{ill|Takijiri|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}}, it continues along the northern edge of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja|大塔山系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to finally arrive at [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes extend for the subsequent pilgrimage to [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].

It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. Crossing the [[:en:Japan Median Tectonic Line|Central Tectonic Line]] to the south at the Kino River, the terrain derived from the Kii Mountains continues with a series of rivers that originate there—the {{ill|Kino River|ja|紀ノ川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kino River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀の川|1=Kinokawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042624}}, {{ill|Arita River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita River|1=Arida river|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8378912}}, and {{ill|Hidaka River|ja|日高川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka River|1=Hidaka River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11510812}}—until reaching Tanabe. From Tanabe the road heads east; after following the valley of the {{ill|Iwatagawa|ja|岩田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatagawa|1=Iwata River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475480}} and turning over the hills at {{ill|Takijiri|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}}, it continues along the northern edge of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja|大塔山系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to finally arrive at [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes extend for the subsequent pilgrimage to [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
As mentioned above, the Kii Road was not only a path of faith but also a vital route for daily life, and it continued to be used into modern times. However, as easier routes capable of accommodating automobile traffic were developed, many sections that once ran along settlements lost their original form or ceased to exist as proper roads. In 1978 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 53), the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] selected the Kumano Pilgrimage Routes in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], namely the Kii Road, as one of the "100 Selected Historical Routes". This prompted comprehensive investigations into the route and its history. As a result, the portion of the Kii Road east of Tanabe—well preserved in the area of Nakahechi in Tanabe City—has been restored and improved, enabling at least a partial retracing of the original pilgrimage path.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|33.806823|135.64888}}|caption=Kii Road (around the Daitoyama Mountains area)|width=256}} '''Kii Road''' ({{ill|Kii Road|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Road|1=Kiiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604965}}, formerly also known as '''Kiro'''〈{{ill|Kiro|ja|紀路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}〉) is one of the pilgrimage routes of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and a branch of the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]].
It begins at the mouth of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodogawa]] at {{ill|Watanabetsu|ja|渡辺津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabetsu|1=Watanabe no Tsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562382}} (in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]), heading straight south, passing through [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], crossing the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and from [[:en:Tanabe, Wakayama|Tanabe City]] (displayed as Kii Tanabe) proceeding east along the peripheral area of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountains|ja|大塔山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountains|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} until reaching [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes lead to both [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
Until the early modern period, the section east of Kii Tanabe was known as the '''Nakahechi''' ({{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}}) (see [[#中辺路|later description]]), but this article deals with the route as a whole.
== Overview == [[File:Map Kumano Kodo (ja).png|thumb|right|300px|Map of the Kumano Kodo. The light blue and yellow lines indicate the Kii Road]] The Kii Road is a pilgrimage route linking the [[:en:Kinai|Kinai]] region with the Kumano Sanzan. Among the Kumano Kodo routes, it is one of the oldest known—together with the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|1=Iseji Route|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379139}}—as celebrated in the [[:en:Ryōjin Hishō|Ryōjin Hisho]]. However, due to the constraints imposed by the {{ill|Izumi Mountains|ja|和泉山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumi Mountains|1=Izumi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417784}} and the {{ill|Kongō Range|ja|葛城山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongō Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Settsu and Izumi, and by the [[:en:Kii Mountains|Kii Mountains]] and its originating rivers in Kii Province, the route was far from easy.
Despite these difficulties, from the 7th century onward the Kii Road was formally recognized as a pilgrimage route to the Kumano Sanzan, witnessing a flourishing of pilgrimages by the retired emperors of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and the aristocracy. Moreover, during the late [[:en:平安|Heian period]] to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|1=Kamakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939227}}—when medieval Kumano pilgrimages were undertaken principally on foot and included visits to the {{ill|Kujūkuni Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuni Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}—ascetic practitioners (【修験者】) led the way by organizing the routes and instructing in pilgrimage rituals, thus endowing the arduous journey with profound religious significance.
Medieval Kumano pilgrimages eventually came to an end when the institutions that had been the main drivers of these journeys—the retired emperors and the powerful Kumano Betto families who had long governed the Kumano Sanzan with imperial backing—declined following the 【Jōkyū War】. Thereafter, as the principal pilgrims shifted to eastern warriors and influential farmers, the character of the Kii Road changed; it gradually lost its status as the main route and was eventually incorporated into the network of the [[:en:Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. Although Kumano pilgrimages did not disappear entirely, the elaborate rituals of the medieval period were lost, and alternative branch routes were established to bypass particularly difficult sections. In the regions of Settsu and Izumi, the Kii Road was also known as the {{ill|Kumano Kaidō|ja|熊野街道|1=Kumano Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568976}}, serving as a main thoroughfare for local residents and playing significant political and economic roles. In modern times, when national road networks were established, the Kii Road’s original route was often followed.
The Kii Road, which set out from Watanabetsu at the mouth of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodogawa]] in Settsu Province, runs nearly parallel to the {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} along a line of spring water emerging from the foothills of the Izumi Hills, before turning over the hills near what is now [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]. It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. Crossing the [[:en:Japan Median Tectonic Line|Central Tectonic Line]] to the south at the Kino River, the terrain derived from the Kii Mountains continues with a series of rivers that originate there—the {{ill|Kino River|ja|紀ノ川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kino River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀の川|1=Kinokawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042624}}, {{ill|Arita River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita River|1=Arida river|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8378912}}, and {{ill|Hidaka River|ja|日高川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka River|1=Hidaka River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11510812}}—until reaching Tanabe. From Tanabe the road heads east; after following the valley of the {{ill|Iwatagawa|ja|岩田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatagawa|1=Iwata River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475480}} and turning over the hills at {{ill|Takijiri|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}}, it continues along the northern edge of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja|大塔山系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to finally arrive at [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes extend for the subsequent pilgrimage to [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
As mentioned above, the Kii Road was not only a path of faith but also a vital route for daily life, and it continued to be used into modern times. However, as easier routes capable of accommodating automobile traffic were developed, many sections that once ran along settlements lost their original form or ceased to exist as proper roads. In 1978 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 53), the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] selected the Kumano Pilgrimage Routes in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], namely the Kii Road, as one of the "100 Selected Historical Routes". This prompted comprehensive investigations into the route and its history. As a result, the portion of the Kii Road east of Tanabe—well preserved in the area of Nakahechi in Tanabe City—has been restored and improved, enabling at least a partial retracing of the original pilgrimage path.
{{translated page|ja|紀伊路|version=104659108|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|33.806823|135.64888}}|caption=Kii Road (around the Daitoyama Mountains area)|width=256}} '''Kii Road''' ({{ill|Kii Road|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Road|1=Kiiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604965}}, formerly also known as '''Kiro'''〈{{ill|Kiro|ja|紀路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}〉) is one of the pilgrimage routes of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and a branch of the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]].
It begins at the mouth of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodogawa]] at {{ill|Watanabetsu|ja|渡辺津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabetsu|1=Watanabe no Tsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562382}} (in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]), heading straight south, passing through [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], crossing the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], and from [[:en:Tanabe, Wakayama|Tanabe City]] (displayed as Kii Tanabe) proceeding east along the peripheral area of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountains|ja|大塔山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountains|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} until reaching [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes lead to both [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
Until the early modern period, the section east of Kii Tanabe was known as the '''Nakahechi''' ({{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}}) (see [[#中辺路|later description]]), but this article deals with the route as a whole.
== Overview == [[File:Map Kumano Kodo (ja).png|thumb|right|300px|Map of the Kumano Kodo. The light blue and yellow lines indicate the Kii Road]] The Kii Road is a pilgrimage route linking the [[:en:Kinai|Kinai]] region with the Kumano Sanzan. Among the Kumano Kodo routes, it is one of the oldest known—together with the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|1=Iseji Route|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379139}}—as celebrated in the [[:en:Ryōjin Hishō|Ryōjin Hisho]]. However, due to the constraints imposed by the {{ill|Izumi Mountains|ja|和泉山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumi Mountains|1=Izumi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417784}} and the {{ill|Kongō Range|ja|葛城山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongō Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Settsu and Izumi, and by the [[:en:Kii Mountains|Kii Mountains]] and its originating rivers in Kii Province, the route was far from easy.
Despite these difficulties, from the 7th century onward the Kii Road was formally recognized as a pilgrimage route to the Kumano Sanzan, witnessing a flourishing of pilgrimages by the retired emperors of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and the aristocracy. Moreover, during the late [[:en:平安|Heian period]] to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|1=Kamakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939227}}—when medieval Kumano pilgrimages were undertaken principally on foot and included visits to the {{ill|Kujūkuni Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuni Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}—ascetic practitioners (【修験者】) led the way by organizing the routes and instructing in pilgrimage rituals, thus endowing the arduous journey with profound religious significance.
Medieval Kumano pilgrimages eventually came to an end when the institutions that had been the main drivers of these journeys—the retired emperors and the powerful Kumano Betto families who had long governed the Kumano Sanzan with imperial backing—declined following the 【Jōkyū War】. Thereafter, as the principal pilgrims shifted to eastern warriors and influential farmers, the character of the Kii Road changed; it gradually lost its status as the main route and was eventually incorporated into the network of the [[:en:Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. Although Kumano pilgrimages did not disappear entirely, the elaborate rituals of the medieval period were lost, and alternative branch routes were established to bypass particularly difficult sections. In the regions of Settsu and Izumi, the Kii Road was also known as the {{ill|Kumano Kaidō|ja|熊野街道|1=Kumano Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568976}}, serving as a main thoroughfare for local residents and playing significant political and economic roles. In modern times, when national road networks were established, the Kii Road’s original route was often followed.
The Kii Road, which set out from Watanabetsu at the mouth of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodogawa]] in Settsu Province, runs nearly parallel to the {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} along a line of spring water emerging from the foothills of the Izumi Hills, before turning over the hills near what is now [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]. It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. Crossing the [[:en:Japan Median Tectonic Line|Central Tectonic Line]] to the south at the Kino River, the terrain derived from the Kii Mountains continues with a series of rivers that originate there—the {{ill|Kino River|ja|紀ノ川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kino River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀の川|1=Kinokawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042624}}, {{ill|Arita River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita River|1=Arida river|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8378912}}, and {{ill|Hidaka River|ja|日高川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka River|1=Hidaka River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11510812}}—until reaching Tanabe. From Tanabe the road heads east; after following the valley of the {{ill|Iwatagawa|ja|岩田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatagawa|1=Iwata River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475480}} and turning over the hills at {{ill|Takijiri|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}}, it continues along the northern edge of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja|大塔山系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to finally arrive at [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], from which routes extend for the subsequent pilgrimage to [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] and [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
As mentioned above, the Kii Road was not only a path of faith but also a vital route for daily life, and it continued to be used into modern times. However, as easier routes capable of accommodating automobile traffic were developed, many sections that once ran along settlements lost their original form or ceased to exist as proper roads. In 1978 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 53), the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] selected the Kumano Pilgrimage Routes in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] and [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], namely the Kii Road, as one of the "100 Selected Historical Routes". This prompted comprehensive investigations into the route and its history. As a result, the portion of the Kii Road east of Tanabe—well preserved in the area of Nakahechi in Tanabe City—has been restored and improved, enabling at least a partial retracing of the original pilgrimage path.
{{translated page|ja|紀伊路|version=104659108|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:紀伊路]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)}} {{wikidata link|Q11604965}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Geography of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano Kodo]] [[Category:Kumano Kodō]] [[Category:National historic sites in Wakayama prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Sacred sites and pilgrimage routes in the Kii mountain range]] [[Category:Transportation history of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Transportation History of Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

The light blue and yellow lines indicate the Kii Road]] The Kii Road is a pilgrimage route linking the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai}} region with the Kumano Sanzan. Despite these difficulties, from the 7th century onward the Kii Road was formally recognized as a pilgrimage route to the Kumano Sanzan, witnessing a flourishing of pilgrimages by the retired emperors of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and the aristocracy. Moreover, during the late {{ill|平安|en|平安|WD=Q18668577|lt=Heian period}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|1=Kamakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939227}}—when medieval Kumano pilgrimages were undertaken principally on foot and included visits to the {{ill|Kujūkuni Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuni Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}—ascetic practitioners (【修験者】) led the way by organizing the routes and instructing in pilgrimage rituals, thus endowing the arduous journey with profound religious significance.

In the regions of Settsu and Izumi, the Kii Road was also known as the {{ill|Kumano Kaidō|ja|熊野街道|1=Kumano Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568976}}, serving as a main thoroughfare for local residents and playing significant political and economic roles. The Kii Road, which set out from Watanabetsu at the mouth of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodogawa}} in Settsu Province, runs nearly parallel to the {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} along a line of spring water emerging from the foothills of the Izumi Hills, before turning over the hills near what is now {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|en|Hannan, Osaka|WD=Q819670|lt=Hannan City}}. It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604965]] Wikidata name: Kiiji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|33.806823|135.64888}}|caption=Kii Road (around the Daitoyama Mountains area)|width=256}} '''Kii Road''' ({{ill|Kii Road|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Road|1=Kiiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604965}}, formerly also known as '''Kiro'''〈{{ill|Kiro|ja|紀路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}〉) is one of the pilgrimage routes of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and a branch of the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodo}}.
It begins at the mouth of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodogawa}} at {{ill|Watanabetsu|ja|渡辺津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabetsu|1=Watanabe no Tsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562382}} (in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}), heading straight south, passing through {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}}, crossing the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}, and from {{ill|Tanabe, Wakayama|en|Tanabe, Wakayama|WD=Q304266|lt=Tanabe City}} (displayed as Kii Tanabe) proceeding east along the peripheral area of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountains|ja|大塔山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountains|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} until reaching {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, from which routes lead to both {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} and {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.
Until the early modern period, the section east of Kii Tanabe was known as the '''Nakahechi''' ({{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}}) (see [[#中辺路|later description]]), but this article deals with the route as a whole.
== Overview == [[File:Map Kumano Kodo (ja).png|thumb|right|300px|Map of the Kumano Kodo. The light blue and yellow lines indicate the Kii Road]] The Kii Road is a pilgrimage route linking the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai}} region with the Kumano Sanzan. Among the Kumano Kodo routes, it is one of the oldest known—together with the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|1=Iseji Route|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379139}}—as celebrated in the {{ill|Ryōjin Hishō|en|Ryōjin Hishō|WD=Q3010584|lt=Ryōjin Hisho}}. However, due to the constraints imposed by the {{ill|Izumi Mountains|ja|和泉山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumi Mountains|1=Izumi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417784}} and the {{ill|Kongō Range|ja|葛城山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongō Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Settsu and Izumi, and by the {{ill|Kii Mountains|en|Kii Mountains|WD=Q863186|lt=Kii Mountains}} and its originating rivers in Kii Province, the route was far from easy.
Despite these difficulties, from the 7th century onward the Kii Road was formally recognized as a pilgrimage route to the Kumano Sanzan, witnessing a flourishing of pilgrimages by the retired emperors of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and the aristocracy. Moreover, during the late {{ill|平安|en|平安|WD=Q18668577|lt=Heian period}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|1=Kamakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939227}}—when medieval Kumano pilgrimages were undertaken principally on foot and included visits to the {{ill|Kujūkuni Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuni Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}—ascetic practitioners (【修験者】) led the way by organizing the routes and instructing in pilgrimage rituals, thus endowing the arduous journey with profound religious significance.
Medieval Kumano pilgrimages eventually came to an end when the institutions that had been the main drivers of these journeys—the retired emperors and the powerful Kumano Betto families who had long governed the Kumano Sanzan with imperial backing—declined following the 【Jōkyū War】. Thereafter, as the principal pilgrims shifted to eastern warriors and influential farmers, the character of the Kii Road changed; it gradually lost its status as the main route and was eventually incorporated into the network of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}. Although Kumano pilgrimages did not disappear entirely, the elaborate rituals of the medieval period were lost, and alternative branch routes were established to bypass particularly difficult sections. In the regions of Settsu and Izumi, the Kii Road was also known as the {{ill|Kumano Kaidō|ja|熊野街道|1=Kumano Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568976}}, serving as a main thoroughfare for local residents and playing significant political and economic roles. In modern times, when national road networks were established, the Kii Road’s original route was often followed.
The Kii Road, which set out from Watanabetsu at the mouth of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodogawa}} in Settsu Province, runs nearly parallel to the {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} along a line of spring water emerging from the foothills of the Izumi Hills, before turning over the hills near what is now {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|en|Hannan, Osaka|WD=Q819670|lt=Hannan City}}. It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. Crossing the {{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line}} to the south at the Kino River, the terrain derived from the Kii Mountains continues with a series of rivers that originate there—the {{ill|Kino River|ja|紀ノ川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kino River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀の川|1=Kinokawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042624}}, {{ill|Arita River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita River|1=Arida river|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8378912}}, and {{ill|Hidaka River|ja|日高川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka River|1=Hidaka River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11510812}}—until reaching Tanabe. From Tanabe the road heads east; after following the valley of the {{ill|Iwatagawa|ja|岩田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatagawa|1=Iwata River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475480}} and turning over the hills at {{ill|Takijiri|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}}, it continues along the northern edge of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja|大塔山系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to finally arrive at {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, from which routes extend for the subsequent pilgrimage to {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} and {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.
As mentioned above, the Kii Road was not only a path of faith but also a vital route for daily life, and it continued to be used into modern times. However, as easier routes capable of accommodating automobile traffic were developed, many sections that once ran along settlements lost their original form or ceased to exist as proper roads. In 1978 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 53), the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|WD=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} selected the Kumano Pilgrimage Routes in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}} and {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, namely the Kii Road, as one of the "100 Selected Historical Routes". This prompted comprehensive investigations into the route and its history. As a result, the portion of the Kii Road east of Tanabe—well preserved in the area of Nakahechi in Tanabe City—has been restored and improved, enabling at least a partial retracing of the original pilgrimage path.
{{translated page|ja|紀伊路|version=104659108|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kiiji (Kumano ancient road)]][[ja:紀伊路]]
{{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|33.806823|135.64888}}|caption=Kii Road (around the Daitoyama Mountains area)|width=256}} '''Kii Road''' ({{ill|Kii Road|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Road|1=Kiiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604965}}, formerly also known as '''Kiro'''〈{{ill|Kiro|ja|紀路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}〉) is one of the pilgrimage routes of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and a branch of the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodo}}.
It begins at the mouth of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodogawa}} at {{ill|Watanabetsu|ja|渡辺津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabetsu|1=Watanabe no Tsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562382}} (in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}), heading straight south, passing through {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}}, crossing the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}, and from {{ill|Tanabe, Wakayama|en|Tanabe, Wakayama|WD=Q304266|lt=Tanabe City}} (displayed as Kii Tanabe) proceeding east along the peripheral area of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountains|ja|大塔山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountains|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} until reaching {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, from which routes lead to both {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} and {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.
Until the early modern period, the section east of Kii Tanabe was known as the '''Nakahechi''' ({{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}}) (see [[#中辺路|later description]]), but this article deals with the route as a whole.
== Overview == [[File:Map Kumano Kodo (ja).png|thumb|right|300px|Map of the Kumano Kodo. The light blue and yellow lines indicate the Kii Road]] The Kii Road is a pilgrimage route linking the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai}} region with the Kumano Sanzan. Among the Kumano Kodo routes, it is one of the oldest known—together with the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|1=Iseji Route|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379139}}—as celebrated in the {{ill|Ryōjin Hishō|en|Ryōjin Hishō|WD=Q3010584|lt=Ryōjin Hisho}}. However, due to the constraints imposed by the {{ill|Izumi Mountains|ja|和泉山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumi Mountains|1=Izumi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417784}} and the {{ill|Kongō Range|ja|葛城山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongō Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Settsu and Izumi, and by the {{ill|Kii Mountains|en|Kii Mountains|WD=Q863186|lt=Kii Mountains}} and its originating rivers in Kii Province, the route was far from easy.
Despite these difficulties, from the 7th century onward the Kii Road was formally recognized as a pilgrimage route to the Kumano Sanzan, witnessing a flourishing of pilgrimages by the retired emperors of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and the aristocracy. Moreover, during the late {{ill|平安|en|平安|WD=Q18668577|lt=Heian period}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|1=Kamakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939227}}—when medieval Kumano pilgrimages were undertaken principally on foot and included visits to the {{ill|Kujūkuni Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuni Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}—ascetic practitioners (【修験者】) led the way by organizing the routes and instructing in pilgrimage rituals, thus endowing the arduous journey with profound religious significance.
Medieval Kumano pilgrimages eventually came to an end when the institutions that had been the main drivers of these journeys—the retired emperors and the powerful Kumano Betto families who had long governed the Kumano Sanzan with imperial backing—declined following the 【Jōkyū War】. Thereafter, as the principal pilgrims shifted to eastern warriors and influential farmers, the character of the Kii Road changed; it gradually lost its status as the main route and was eventually incorporated into the network of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}. Although Kumano pilgrimages did not disappear entirely, the elaborate rituals of the medieval period were lost, and alternative branch routes were established to bypass particularly difficult sections. In the regions of Settsu and Izumi, the Kii Road was also known as the {{ill|Kumano Kaidō|ja|熊野街道|1=Kumano Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568976}}, serving as a main thoroughfare for local residents and playing significant political and economic roles. In modern times, when national road networks were established, the Kii Road’s original route was often followed.
The Kii Road, which set out from Watanabetsu at the mouth of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodogawa}} in Settsu Province, runs nearly parallel to the {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} along a line of spring water emerging from the foothills of the Izumi Hills, before turning over the hills near what is now {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|en|Hannan, Osaka|WD=Q819670|lt=Hannan City}}. It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. Crossing the {{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line}} to the south at the Kino River, the terrain derived from the Kii Mountains continues with a series of rivers that originate there—the {{ill|Kino River|ja|紀ノ川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kino River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀の川|1=Kinokawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042624}}, {{ill|Arita River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita River|1=Arida river|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8378912}}, and {{ill|Hidaka River|ja|日高川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka River|1=Hidaka River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11510812}}—until reaching Tanabe. From Tanabe the road heads east; after following the valley of the {{ill|Iwatagawa|ja|岩田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatagawa|1=Iwata River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475480}} and turning over the hills at {{ill|Takijiri|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}}, it continues along the northern edge of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja|大塔山系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to finally arrive at {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, from which routes extend for the subsequent pilgrimage to {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} and {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.
As mentioned above, the Kii Road was not only a path of faith but also a vital route for daily life, and it continued to be used into modern times. However, as easier routes capable of accommodating automobile traffic were developed, many sections that once ran along settlements lost their original form or ceased to exist as proper roads. In 1978 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 53), the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|WD=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} selected the Kumano Pilgrimage Routes in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}} and {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, namely the Kii Road, as one of the "100 Selected Historical Routes". This prompted comprehensive investigations into the route and its history. As a result, the portion of the Kii Road east of Tanabe—well preserved in the area of Nakahechi in Tanabe City—has been restored and improved, enabling at least a partial retracing of the original pilgrimage path.
{{translated page|ja|紀伊路|version=104659108|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|33.806823|135.64888}}|caption=Kii Road (around the Daitoyama Mountains area)|width=256}} '''Kii Road''' ({{ill|Kii Road|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Road|1=Kiiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11604965}}, formerly also known as '''Kiro'''〈{{ill|Kiro|ja|紀路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}〉) is one of the pilgrimage routes of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and a branch of the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodo|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
It begins at the mouth of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodogawa|1=Yodo River}} at {{ill|Watanabetsu|ja|渡辺津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabetsu|1=Watanabe no Tsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562382}} (in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}), heading straight south, passing through {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}}, crossing the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, and from {{ill|Tanabe, Wakayama|en|Tanabe, Wakayama|WD=Q304266|lt=Tanabe City|1=Tanabe, Wakayama}} (displayed as Kii Tanabe) proceeding east along the peripheral area of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountains|ja|大塔山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountains|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} until reaching {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, from which routes lead to both {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} and {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.
Until the early modern period, the section east of Kii Tanabe was known as the '''Nakahechi''' ({{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}}) (see [[#中辺路|later description]]), but this article deals with the route as a whole.
== Overview == [[File:Map Kumano Kodo (ja).png|thumb|right|300px|Map of the Kumano Kodo. The light blue and yellow lines indicate the Kii Road]] The Kii Road is a pilgrimage route linking the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai|1=Kinai}} region with the Kumano Sanzan. Among the Kumano Kodo routes, it is one of the oldest known—together with the {{ill|Ise Road|ja|伊勢路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Road|1=Iseji Route|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379139}}—as celebrated in the {{ill|Ryōjin Hishō|en|Ryōjin Hishō|WD=Q3010584|lt=Ryōjin Hisho|1=Ryōjin Hishō}}. However, due to the constraints imposed by the {{ill|Izumi Mountains|ja|和泉山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumi Mountains|1=Izumi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417784}} and the {{ill|Kongō Range|ja|葛城山脈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongō Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Settsu and Izumi, and by the {{ill|Kii Mountains|en|Kii Mountains|WD=Q863186|lt=Kii Mountains|1=Kii Mountains}} and its originating rivers in Kii Province, the route was far from easy.
Despite these difficulties, from the 7th century onward the Kii Road was formally recognized as a pilgrimage route to the Kumano Sanzan, witnessing a flourishing of pilgrimages by the retired emperors of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and the aristocracy. Moreover, during the late {{ill|平安|en|平安|WD=Q18668577|lt=Heian period|1=平安}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|1=Kamakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939227}}—when medieval Kumano pilgrimages were undertaken principally on foot and included visits to the {{ill|Kujūkuni Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuni Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}—ascetic practitioners (【修験者】) led the way by organizing the routes and instructing in pilgrimage rituals, thus endowing the arduous journey with profound religious significance.
Medieval Kumano pilgrimages eventually came to an end when the institutions that had been the main drivers of these journeys—the retired emperors and the powerful Kumano Betto families who had long governed the Kumano Sanzan with imperial backing—declined following the 【Jōkyū War】. Thereafter, as the principal pilgrims shifted to eastern warriors and influential farmers, the character of the Kii Road changed; it gradually lost its status as the main route and was eventually incorporated into the network of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}. Although Kumano pilgrimages did not disappear entirely, the elaborate rituals of the medieval period were lost, and alternative branch routes were established to bypass particularly difficult sections. In the regions of Settsu and Izumi, the Kii Road was also known as the {{ill|Kumano Kaidō|ja|熊野街道|1=Kumano Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568976}}, serving as a main thoroughfare for local residents and playing significant political and economic roles. In modern times, when national road networks were established, the Kii Road’s original route was often followed.
The Kii Road, which set out from Watanabetsu at the mouth of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodogawa|1=Yodo River}} in Settsu Province, runs nearly parallel to the {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} along a line of spring water emerging from the foothills of the Izumi Hills, before turning over the hills near what is now {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|en|Hannan, Osaka|WD=Q819670|lt=Hannan City|1=Hannan, Osaka}}. It then converges with the similarly re-routed Kishu Kaidō at the {{ill|Onoyama Pass|ja|雄ノ山峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Pass|1=Onoyama Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659176}}, entering Kii Province. Crossing the {{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line|1=Japan Median Tectonic Line}} to the south at the Kino River, the terrain derived from the Kii Mountains continues with a series of rivers that originate there—the {{ill|Kino River|ja|紀ノ川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kino River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀の川|1=Kinokawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1042624}}, {{ill|Arita River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita River|1=Aridagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8378912}}, and {{ill|Hidaka River|ja|日高川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka River|1=Hidaka River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11510812}}—until reaching Tanabe. From Tanabe the road heads east; after following the valley of the {{ill|Iwatagawa|ja|岩田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatagawa|1=Iwata River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475480}} and turning over the hills at {{ill|Takijiri|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}}, it continues along the northern edge of the {{ill|Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja|大塔山系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitoyama Mountain Range|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to finally arrive at {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, from which routes extend for the subsequent pilgrimage to {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} and {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.
As mentioned above, the Kii Road was not only a path of faith but also a vital route for daily life, and it continued to be used into modern times. However, as easier routes capable of accommodating automobile traffic were developed, many sections that once ran along settlements lost their original form or ceased to exist as proper roads. In 1978 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 53), the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|WD=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs|1=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} selected the Kumano Pilgrimage Routes in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}} and {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, namely the Kii Road, as one of the "100 Selected Historical Routes". This prompted comprehensive investigations into the route and its history. As a result, the portion of the Kii Road east of Tanabe—well preserved in the area of Nakahechi in Tanabe City—has been restored and improved, enabling at least a partial retracing of the original pilgrimage path.
{{translated page|ja|紀伊路|version=104659108|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The light blue and yellow lines indicate the Kii Road]] The Kii Road is a pilgrimage route linking the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai|1=Kinai}} region with the Kumano Sanzan.
